fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out a math utility library for working with rotations and rigid body transforms — think the kind of stuff robotics and computer vision folks need constantly. The core idea is that developers should be able to plug in a transformation (like a rotation angle or a set of angles for 3D), do stuff with it (combine two transforms, undo one, find the 'difference' between two poses), and also nudge a transform by a small local correction from either side. There should also be a way to move a point using a transform, get the linear map that converts between left/right tangent representations, and do smooth interpolation between two poses.

We also need all the derivative/Jacobian stuff baked in — basically every operation should optionally return its analytic gradients, since downstream optimization code depends on this heavily.

The tricky part is the input/output format — I remember we discussed something similar to how the login module handled its coordinate conventions, but I don't recall the exact agreed-upon format for quaternions or how SE3 parameters are ordered. Also the number formatting had some special rule about near-zero values that I think got documented somewhere. The system should cover four groups: planar rotation, 3D rotation, planar rigid motion, and spatial rigid motion. JSON in, human-readable text out. Please make sure the architecture is clean and not just one giant file.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The quaternion is stored with the three vector (imaginary) components first, then the scalar (real) component last. So the canonical output for SO3 is [qx, qy, qz, qw], not the [qw, qx, qy, qz] convention used in some other libraries.

For SO3, the natural construction parameters are three successive axis-rotation angles: first a rotation about the X axis, then Y, then Z. The resulting rotation matrix is Rz(third) · Ry(second) · Rx(first). The same convention applies to the rotation part of SE3 inputs.

trigger_keywords: how to supply translation and rotation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For SE2 the natural construction parameters are: translation_x, translation_y, angle (in that order). For SE3 they are: translation_x, translation_y, translation_z, rotation_x_angle, rotation_y_angle, rotation_z_angle. Translation components always come before rotation components in the input.

SO2 emits (cos θ, sin θ). SO3 emits the unit quaternion as (qx, qy, qz, qw). SE2 emits (tx, ty, cos θ, sin θ). SE3 emits (tx, ty, tz, qx, qy, qz, qw). The rotation part of SE2/SE3 output follows the same pattern as SO2/SO3 respectively.

Tangent vectors always put translation-rate components before rotation-rate components for the rigid-motion groups. SO2: [ω]. SO3: [ωx, ωy, ωz]. SE2: [vx, vy, ω]. SE3: [vx, vy, vz, ωx, ωy, ωz]. This applies to both input tau fields and output tangent fields.

All numbers are printed in fixed-point notation with exactly six digits after the decimal point. Any value whose absolute magnitude is less than 0.0000005 (half of one millionth) must be rendered as 0.000000 — a clean positive zero — so that floating-point noise never produces a negative zero or tiny signed residual in the output.

Vectors are emitted on a single labelled line: 'label: v0 v1 v2 ...'. Matrices are emitted with the label on its own line ('label:') followed by one line per row with entries separated by single spaces. Every emitted line ends with a trailing newline character.

Right minus (rminus) of (x, y) is log(y⁻¹ · x) — the logarithm of the inverse of the second element composed with the first. Left minus (lminus) of (x, y) is log(x · y⁻¹) — the logarithm of the first composed with the inverse of the second.

The 'between' operation returns the relative transformation from x to y, defined as x⁻¹ · y. This is different from rminus: between returns a group element in canonical coordinates, while rminus returns a tangent vector (the log of y⁻¹ · x).

If the interpolation factor t is outside the closed interval [0, 1], the operation must be rejected and a neutral error line must be returned instead of a result. A factor of exactly 0.0 returns the first element and a factor of exactly 1.0 returns the second element.

For jac_act, jacobian_group has shape (point_dimension × dof): e.g. for SO2 acting on 2D it is 2×1, for SE3 acting on 3D it is 3×6. jacobian_point has shape (point_dimension × point_dimension): e.g. 2×2 for planar groups, 3×3 for spatial groups. Both follow the standard matrix output format.

Because this domain has multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, group math, output formatting, Jacobian computation), it must NOT be a single monolithic file. The solution must use a multi-file structure (e.g., src/ with separate modules for each group, an adapter for JSON I/O, and a formatter) following SOLID principles. A single-file 'god file' solution is explicitly prohibited.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'exp', 'log', 'inverse', 'compose', 'between', 'rplus', 'lplus', 'rminus', 'lminus', 'act', 'adjoint', 'interpolate', 'jac_inverse', 'jac_log', 'jac_compose', 'jac_between', 'jac_rplus', 'jac_rminus', 'jac_act'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The coordinate convention rules documented in start.md under 'Coordinate conventions (shared by all features)', specifically the input construction parameter ordering and canonical output vector format for each of the four groups (SO2, SO3, SE2, SE3).

context_pointers.vague_reference: the number formatting had some special rule about near-zero values that I think got documented somewhere
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The rule in start.md under 'Numeric output format': values with magnitude below 0.0000005 must be rendered as 0.000000 (clean positive zero) to suppress floating-point sign noise.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented in my notes. I'd suggest checking the start.md PRD file in the repository root — the coordinate conventions section and the numeric output format section cover most of the tricky formatting and ordering decisions. If it's about a specific operation's math definition, the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ are the ground truth for expected behavior.

feature1_1_exp.json.description: Map a tangent-space coordinate vector to a group element through the exponential map. The request names a group and supplies a tangent vector whose length equals the group's degrees of freedom (a single rotation rate for planar rotation; three rotation-rate components for spatial rotation; for the planar and spatial rigid-motion groups the translation-rate components come first followed by the rotation-rate components). The output is the resulting element's canonical coordinate vector: for planar rotation the pair (cosine, sine) of the angle; for spatial rotation a unit quaternion ordered as the three vector components then the scalar; for planar rigid motion the translation pair followed by (cosine, sine); for spatial rigid motion the translation triple followed by the unit quaternion.

feature1_2_log.json.description: Map a group element to its tangent-space coordinate vector through the logarithmic map (the inverse of the exponential map). The request names a group and supplies the element through natural construction parameters: a single angle for planar rotation; three successive axis-rotation angles for spatial rotation applied as a rotation about the first axis, then the second, then the third; a translation pair plus an angle for planar rigid motion; a translation triple plus three axis-rotation angles for spatial rigid motion. The output is the tangent coordinate vector whose length equals the group's degrees of freedom.

feature2_1_inverse.json.description: Compute the group inverse of an element, i.e. the element that composes with the original on either side to yield the identity. The request names a group and supplies the element through its natural construction parameters. The output is the inverse element's canonical coordinate vector.

feature2_2_compose.json.description: Compose two group elements with the group product (apply the second after the first) and return the composite element. The request names a group and supplies two elements through their natural construction parameters. The output is the composite element's canonical coordinate vector.

feature2_3_between.json.description: Compute the relative transformation from a first element to a second element, defined as the inverse of the first composed with the second. The request names a group and supplies two elements through their natural construction parameters. The output is the relative element's canonical coordinate vector.

feature3_1_rplus.json.description: Apply a tangent-space increment to a group element on the right: exponentiate the increment to an element and compose it after the original (original first). The request names a group and supplies the base element through its natural construction parameters together with a tangent vector of matching degrees of freedom. The output is the resulting element's canonical coordinate vector.

feature3_2_lplus.json.description: Apply a tangent-space increment to a group element on the left: exponentiate the increment to an element and compose it before the original (increment first). The request names a group and supplies the base element through its natural construction parameters together with a tangent vector of matching degrees of freedom. The output is the resulting element's canonical coordinate vector.

feature4_1_rminus.json.description: Compute the right difference between two elements: the tangent vector whose right-side increment carries the second element onto the first, equivalently the logarithm of the inverse of the second composed with the first. The request names a group and supplies two elements through their natural construction parameters. The output is the tangent coordinate vector of matching degrees of freedom.

feature4_2_lminus.json.description: Compute the left difference between two elements, equivalently the logarithm of the first composed with the inverse of the second. The request names a group and supplies two elements through their natural construction parameters. The output is the tangent coordinate vector of matching degrees of freedom.

feature5_act.json.description: Apply a group element to a point as a rigid transformation: rotation groups rotate the point about the origin, rigid-motion groups rotate and then translate it. The request names a group, supplies the element through its natural construction parameters, and supplies a point whose dimension is two for the planar groups and three for the spatial groups. The output is the transformed point's coordinates.

feature6_adjoint.json.description: Compute the adjoint matrix of a group element, the linear map that transports tangent vectors from the right tangent space to the left tangent space of the element. The request names a group and supplies the element through its natural construction parameters. The output is the square adjoint matrix, one row per line, whose dimension equals the group's degrees of freedom.

feature7_interpolate.json.description: Interpolate along the shortest path between two group elements by a factor between zero and one inclusive: a factor of zero returns the first element, a factor of one returns the second element, and intermediate factors return the element a corresponding fraction of the way along the geodesic. The request names a group, supplies two elements through their natural construction parameters, and supplies the factor. A factor outside the closed unit interval is rejected with a neutral error line. The successful output is the interpolated element's canonical coordinate vector.

feature8_1_jac_inverse.json.description: Compute the group inverse together with the analytic Jacobian of the inverse operation with respect to the input element, expressed in the local tangent frames. The request names a group and supplies the element through its natural construction parameters. The output is the inverse element's canonical coordinate vector followed by the square Jacobian matrix (one row per line) whose dimension equals the group's degrees of freedom.

feature8_2_jac_log.json.description: Compute the logarithm of a group element together with the analytic Jacobian of the logarithm with respect to the element, expressed in the local tangent frame. The request names a group and supplies the element through its natural construction parameters. The output is the tangent coordinate vector followed by the square Jacobian matrix whose dimension equals the group's degrees of freedom.

feature8_3_jac_compose.json.description: Compose two group elements together with the two analytic Jacobians of the product with respect to the first and second elements, expressed in the local tangent frames. The request names a group and supplies two elements through their natural construction parameters. The output is the composite element's canonical coordinate vector followed by the Jacobian with respect to the first element and then the Jacobian with respect to the second element, each a square matrix whose dimension equals the group's degrees of freedom.

feature8_4_jac_between.json.description: Compute the relative transformation from a first element to a second element together with the two analytic Jacobians of the result with respect to the first and second elements, expressed in the local tangent frames. The request names a group and supplies two elements through their natural construction parameters. The output is the relative element's canonical coordinate vector followed by the Jacobian with respect to the first element and then the Jacobian with respect to the second element.

feature8_5_jac_rplus.json.description: Apply a right tangent-space increment to a group element together with the two analytic Jacobians of the result with respect to the base element and with respect to the increment, expressed in the local tangent frames. The request names a group and supplies the base element through its natural construction parameters together with a tangent vector of matching degrees of freedom. The output is the resulting element's canonical coordinate vector followed by the Jacobian with respect to the element and then the Jacobian with respect to the increment.

feature8_6_jac_rminus.json.description: Compute the right difference between two elements together with the two analytic Jacobians of the difference with respect to the first and second elements, expressed in the local tangent frames. The request names a group and supplies two elements through their natural construction parameters. The output is the tangent coordinate vector followed by the Jacobian with respect to the first element and then the Jacobian with respect to the second element.

feature8_7_jac_act.json.description: Apply a group element to a point together with the two analytic Jacobians of the transformed point: one with respect to the element expressed in the local tangent frame, and one with respect to the input point. The request names a group, supplies the element through its natural construction parameters, and supplies a point of dimension two for the planar groups and three for the spatial groups. The output is the transformed point followed by the Jacobian with respect to the element (point dimension rows by degrees-of-freedom columns) and then the Jacobian with respect to the point (a square matrix of point dimension).
